title: Kitchen Cabinet Sink Base, 36"
summary: A 36"-wide backless sink-base cabinet built from cabinet-grade plywood with a full-overlay face frame — doors and drawer face are a separate project.
description:
A sink base is the one cabinet in a run that's built without a back, and that's exactly what makes the back supports load-bearing rather than decorative: with no back panel tying the sides together, the pair of supports (scrap plywood or 1x3, doesn't matter which) at the top and bottom is what keeps the box from racking every time a door swings. Skip them and the cabinet stays square only until the plumbing goes in and someone leans on the counter above it.

The face frame gets built and clamped as its own flat assembly before it ever touches the carcass — every joint clamped, marked, and glued on a flat surface, then pocket-screwed to the front edges. Building it in place instead invites a frame that's square to nothing. And this plan stops deliberately short of the door and drawer face: they take different tools and know-how, warp more than any other cabinet part, and are cheap enough to have built separately or ordered pre-made — bundling them in here would make every other cabinet in a run repeat the same door-specific instructions.

tools.note: Rips the plywood sheet to the 22-3/4" strip width.

materials.name: Cabinet-grade plywood, 3/4", 4x8 sheet
materials.note: Ripped into a 22-3/4"-wide strip; only one strip is needed but a full sheet must be bought to get it — save the scrap.

materials.name: Scrap plywood or 1x3 furring strips, for back supports

steps.title: Build the base
steps.body: Build the base as shown, 1/2" narrower in width than the cabinet carcass to account for the face-frame overhang. Drill pocket holes now for attaching the base to the underside of the cabinet in a later step, then set the base aside.

steps.title: Build the carcase
steps.body: Rip the plywood sheet to a 22-3/4" strip and build the sides and top into the carcase. Drill 3/4" pocket holes along the front edges for attaching the face frame in a later step.

steps.title: Add the back supports
steps.body: Attach the back supports at the top and back of the carcase — scrap plywood strips or 1x3/1x4 boards both work. With no back panel on a sink base, these supports are what keeps the box square.
materials: Scrap plywood or 1x3 furring strips, for back supports

steps.title: Build the face frame
steps.body: Build the face frame as its own flat assembly: clamp and mark each joint before gluing, and build with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" pocket hole screws. Use fine-thread pocket hole screws if the frame is hardwood, especially where hinges will attach.

steps.title: Add the corner supports
steps.body: Add scrap plywood or 1x3/1x4 corner support pieces where the countertop will land — these give the top plywood something to fasten to once the cabinet run is assembled, and add corner support that helps keep the cabinets square.
materials: Scrap plywood or 1x3 furring strips, for back supports

steps.title: Attach the base
steps.body: Once the carcase is built, attach the base, flush to the back and sides.

steps.title: Attach the face frame
steps.body: Attach the completed face frame to the front of the carcase with glue and pocket hole screws through the pre-drilled front edges.

steps.title: Sand and finish the box
steps.body: Sand every exposed surface and finish. Doors and the drawer face are their own project — build or order them separately and hang once finished.

images.alt: 36-inch kitchen sink base cabinet with a full-overlay face frame
